Here's an out there thought on Pluto waning: I wonder if it's linked to the consumer economy. A consumer economy like one we'd vaguely recognize took form around the 1930s: prior to that, tools were meant as tools, not prosthetics.

Additionally, both Canada and the US, where the consumer economy metastasized so immensely, have the sun in their foundation chart conjunct Pluto's discovery location; it would make sense that the consumer economy would grow to such a ridiculous size in these two nations given such an aspect.

Pluto in mythology represents death; and in Greek mythology, the shades (souls of the dead) were thought to not have anything marking them apart from any other shade. This seems like a good description of a consumer.

Finally, the consumer economy started collapsing around the same time as Pluto was downgraded, so I think there's a very strong case to be made here. While standards of living slowly dropped until then, it wasn't until the Great Recession that people started getting kicked out of the consumer economy in any real way; which seems to suggest a link.

replies.content:
There's a large overlap between the military, communications technology and consumerism as well. The first satellites put up for broadcasting entertainment were funded by Howard Hughes, whose influence was enormous in this sphere. I think the first worldwide broadcast was an Elvis Presley concert, and this was distributed by one of Hughes' satellites.

An interesting book on a similar subject is "Weird Scenes Inside the Canyon" by David McGowan, which details the links between the L.A. counter-cultural music scene and the military-industrial complex. The likes of Jim Morrison, Stephen Stills, Frank Zappa, Grace Slick, David Crosby, Gram Parsons etc. were all the scions of elite military families. Even the drugs they took, such as LSD, were products of the military establishment.

McGowan's book goes off into a conspiracy theory tangent, but what he does show comprehensively is how rock music and the counter-culture were utterly a product of the military-industrial complex.

(Phil Knight)

comments.title: Polytheisy LBRP
comments.content:
I have a few questions regarding the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram for Polytheists. I think it’s mostly concerning just a few clarifications.

1. Following the outline you first posted a while back, near the end when I say: “Before me the powers of Air; behind me the powers of Water; to my right hand the powers of Fire; to my left hand, the powers of Earth.” I feel silly for asking what seems like a dumb question but is East Air, South Fire, West Water, and North Earth?

2. Starting out in the eastern quarter, you state to trace a pentagram, and later “trace a circle around to the south” and then draw another pentagram vibrating another name. I’m not too sure what you mean by trace a circle. Am I tracing a circle in South and drawing the pentagram within the circle? Or is it that I walk to the South corner and trace the pentagram without a circle?

3. Now, assuming the answer to Question #1 is yes, In the LBRP I have had good results with Hera in the East (air), but in the Middle Pillar Exercise, I find Her best suited to the foot center. Is this some contradiction I should sort out, or am I overthinking it?

4. Likewise, in the LBRP I find that, perhaps contrary to popular wisdom, I have Hera as east, (typically earth, but here associated with air?) Zeus as east (typically air but here as fire?), Gaia in the west (typically earth but here as water), and Ares in north(not sure but He feels best here)

Now, assuming the directions and elements are correct as I assume per question 1, how should I experiment with placement here? If I had initial good results for the week I’ve been doing it, should I keep it as is?

5. Regarding visualization. I’ve been poor at keeping up my magical practices and it’s been showing. I’m having trouble not necessarily visualizing the light coming from above and the circle of light around me, but maintaining that visualization as I go through the ritual. I assume this will get better as I practice it daily and take my practice seriously going forward?

Thank you for your time, I appreciate you doing these Magic Mondays!

Jaro (formerly Daniel)

replies.title: Re: Polytheist LBRP
replies.content:
1) Yes.

2) When you do the LBRP, you're tracing a circle around the outside of the space -- from east to south, from south to west, from west to north, and from north back around to east. That's the circle I'm talking about here. It's a horizontal circle, with a pentagram at each of the four quarters -- is that any clearer?

3) You're overthinking it -- and also making a common mistake, in thinking that the positions of the gods in the LBRP have anything to do with the elements. Don't worry about elemental placement in the LBRP -- just get two gods facing each other, and two goddesses facing each other.

4) The same goes here. The fact that you invoke Hera in the east does not mean you're invoking her as a goddess of Air; in the Hermetic LBRP, the name used in the south is Adonai, which is the divine name for Earth! Don't worry about elemental placement in the LBRP. Save that for the Greater Pentagram Rituals.

5) Most people have trouble with visualization at first. Keep practicing and you'll get better.

replies.title: Re: Polytheist LBRP
replies.content: Ah, much clearer, thank you for your help!

replies.title: Re: Polytheisy LBRP
replies.content: You’ve spontaneously hit on the same god/direction attributions as I have. Theophrastus agreed with you about Zeus and Hera, for what that’s worth; ‘Hera’ is etymologically ‘air.’ He had Kore as water.  I would also note that as ‘Mars’ Ares is a farming deity as well as a war deity.

comments.title: Sweetening spells?
comments.content:
Dear JMG and everybody,

This is a question about the use of sweetening spells for people and organisations.

In short: there was a mistake at the specialist health clinic I am using and that caused some serious problems. When asking for help, I run into Nurse Ratched (or her bestie) who clearly enjoyed being mean to someone weaker (me). (sorry, this sounds very strange, but it happened at several occasions.) I got help from somewhere else, but in the future I really need to have a good working relationship with both the nurse and the doctor.

Is a sweetening spell appropriate, given the circumstances? IE a clinic and persons I don't really trust and feel a very strong resentment towards (but need). Furthermore, I need to be a bit vigilant so no more mistakes slips through so given Raspberry Jam Principle, sweet need to be balanced with a worldly saltiness.

Traditionally, people must have used magic for these situations. IE a powerful and somewhat unfair entity that you are still dependent on (administrative government, corporations, feudal lords...).

Doing the DH and DMH but still struggling with the Oghams and therefore don't trust my divination. (Which I guess would be a recommendation otherwise).

Also, can a sweetening spell be directed towards an organisation, or does it have to be a person? And finally, if you allow three questions, when are sweetening spells contra-indicated? (You have already earlier elaborated on the issue of the subject's will, but are there any other considerations?)

Sincerely,

replies.title: Re: Sweetening spells?
replies.content:
1) Yes, that would be an appropriate use for a sweetening spell. The secret of sweetening spells is that you're actually changing your own behavior in subtle ways, to elicit a better response from others, so it's a good ethical choice.

2) Yes, you can aim such a spell at an organization.

3) When a divination suggests not to do them, or when the person or organization is seriously evil and "sweetening" would simply make you more likely to go along with unethical behavior.

comments.title: Levi's Baphomet
comments.content:
Having read Waite's and your translation of Levi's _Dogma and Ritual of High Magic_ I was drawn to the symbolism portrayed by his description and drawing of Baphomet as a divine androgyne intentionally masked as a bogeyman for those easily frightened off (a form of the flake filter, perhaps). However, as my abilities to sense subtle energies have increased as I've worked through CGD, I've started to feel that there is something wrong with the Baphomet egregore. After 150+ years of people twisting it into a symbol of evil ("the devil") and/or inverting its pentagram in French novels and periodicals, Crowley's Thoth deck, Anton LaVey, Black Metal bands, and so forth, I don't feel there is much left there to depict what Levi seems to have intended. It feels like all of their crud has stuck and it has become the negative thing others since Levi have used it for.

I was hoping you might have some thoughts on this matter. More to the point, in your experience, is this still a useful symbol to work with for someone trying to avoid the slimy "vibe" one gets from subtle contact with malign entities or thoughtforms?

As always, thank you for taking the time to host Magic Mondays. I don't ask questions here often, but I always read it and learn a lot.

Wayne /|\

replies.title: Re: Levi's Baphomet
replies.content:
It would take serious magical work to get through the contaminated pop-culture egregor to the essence Levi meant to express. It can be done, though; the way I work with that image is to recall that Baphomet in Hebrew converts via the Atbash cipher (a standard Cabalistic method of converting words) into Sophia, and build a symbolism around the identity of the two.

There's a body of Templar symbolism and ritual that relates to that, and turning that into something others can work with is on my get-to list -- that was one of the reasons I've arranged to receive every Masonic initiation that has some kind of connection to the Templar egregor, and to confer most of them. (If you want to really tap into the subtle forces of a ritual, become the chief officer in a group that performs the ritual...)

comments.title: Talisman symbolism during waning moon
comments.content:
Hello,

I'm planning an elemental talisman to remove excessive self consciousness/social anxiety. Currently, it seems Water is the element that expresses this the best. I'm planning to use the basic ritual of consecration from Circles of Power.

However, I'm unsure if I'm planning this properly for a talisman that removes something. Initally I thought I should make a talisman of Water (with symbols of Water) with the intent to remove social anxiety, and charge it during the waning moon.

Is that the right way to go about it? Years ago when I made an "amulet" (his term for a talisman that removes something) of Mars to remove aggressive energies and fighting in my home (based on instructions from Modern Magic) it seemed to have the opposite effect instead, so I want to make sure I'm not making a mistake with my choice of symbolism for this current talisman.

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Talisman symbolism during waning moon
replies.content:
Um, I'm not surprised you had trouble with the Mars working, and you're setting yourself up for more trouble with a Water talisman. In both cases, you're invoking the thing you want to get rid of, and so getting even more of it. Water is receptive, absorbent, and emotional, so a water talisman will make you more self-conscious and socially anxious, not less! Doing it during the waning moon doesn't change the basic elemental energy; that's one of the things in Kraig's Modern Magic that tends to trip people up.

What you need to do is go to the opposite energy. To remove aggressive energies and fighting, do a Venus talisman -- that's the opposite energy from Mars. To make yourself confident and untroubled in social settings, invoke fire, not water.

replies.title: Re: Talisman symbolism during waning moon
replies.content:
That makes sense, and I was wondering if that was the case after the Mars working turned out the way it did!

I was hoping to do it soon, since the anxiety has been worse lately and the moon is currently waning (and Circles suggests following moon phases). I thought that based on that, I should avoid doing magic to draw something towards me, and could instead banish/send away anxiety, hence my confusion of the symbolism.

Can I still make the Fire talisman now, or will it be best to just wait until the waxing moon?

Thanks for all your help!

replies.title: Re: Talisman symbolism during waning moon
replies.content: Wait for the waxing moon. For this kind of working, it's a better approach.

replies.title: beech tree
replies.content:
Beech trees in a woodland send up little saplings from their roots all around them. I sometimes transplant a few of these saplings from forest on my land to open places, with about a 30% success rate. It is harsh on the sapling to be separated from the roots of its mother tree but they are aggressively browsed by deer anyway, so most of them die young in the forest. In any stand of beeches these saplings will be everywhere around on the forest floor. When transplanting I take as much of the fat root of the mother tree (12") and native soil as I can, then plant with an inoculation of mycorhyza (never spelled this word before, it's probably wrong) available at any gardening supplier.

I'm not sure how often the trees would send up "suckers" if they are in an open place surrounded by grass, but if you look carefully around any edges where the grass is not, such as in flower beds or along fences, there may even be beech trees coming from the mother on your property that you can protect. They look like zig-zaggy silver twigs and the buds at this time of year will be pointy half inch long tightly furled up golden leaves.
Wooler

They also argue that the attributions given to the Invisible Three (and now possibly Invisible Two with Pluto drifting away), seem arbitrary and could just as easily be spread among the Seven.

That seems to raise a bunch of sticky questions. Is astrology an observable science, only in the sense that only what you can observe can have an effect? Even still, we can now observe Uranus and Neptune, and even with some catastrophic deindustrialization, rather than a gradual one, we would still have the technology of optics, which would be useful for all sorts of things. We would still be able to observe and keep track of those distant objects, even if only as a way of remembering the past.

And even with consciousness coming in to play at every level, just because we are not consciously aware of or observing something would not mean it is incapable of having an effect upon us.

At the same time, I can appreciate that there is a balance to traditional astrology. For example, the two luminaries rule Cancer and Leo, and then the five wanderers each have two signs apiece. What would be the basis for taking rulerships from Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn? Especially now that we are theoretically losing Pluto, wouldn't it possibly make sense to give the signs of the luminaries to Neptune and Uranus and give the luminaries special roles as timekeepers? Mythopoetically, one could argue then that their opposition to Saturn's rulerships reflect Saturn's guardianship of the cosmos we can visibly observe. Removing the Sun and Moon from rulerships of the signs might also reflect their unique positions as the Center and our Satellite, as well.

Sorry for the babble, but it's clear that you use the trans-Saturnian planets and I wonder how we balance them with the hundreds and thousands of years that went into just using the Seven and crafting a system balanced on them. Clearly, we lack the hundreds and thousands of years of integrating the trans-Saturnians and may need that time as a species to work it out.

Best,

Jean-Pierre

replies.content: One thing that I've read and found compelling is to consider the trans-Saturnian planets as roughly similar to fixed stars.  This allows to maintain both the incredible symmetry and beauty of traditional astrology while acknowledging the profound influence of the outer planets

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Five thousand years ago, when astrologer-priests realized that there were five stars that moved across the others, I suspect there was a lot of the same kind of argument -- there was this perfectly balanced, elegant system of solar and lunar astrology, and how can you mess it up by dragging in these other planets? Why, all the correspondences of those planets can be assigned either to the sun or the moon, you know...

To my mind, two things have gotten missed here. The first is that any theoretical scheme is merely that, a theoretical scheme constructed by human minds, and the universe is under no obligation to follow it. The second is that, by the basic thesis of astrology itself, the discovery of Uranus and Neptune didn't happen by accident -- these discoveries marked the arrival of new influences in human life, society, and experience, which we'll spend the next five thousand years or so integrating into our lives -- and our systems of astrology.

In the long run, I suspect we'll see an astrology that has three primary factors (sun, moon, and ascendant representing Earth) and seven secondary factors (Mercury through Neptune), with rulerships adjusted in ways that you and I probably can't even guess at yet. For the time being, I accept that the inner planets (Mercury through Mars) rule two signs each and the luminaries and outer planets rule one sign each, and that seems to work in practice, too. But we'll see...

comments.title: The World, and How to Get There
comments.content:
Since childhood I've had this recurring sense/awareness that shows up in one of two opposite modes, and in varying degrees of severity.

On the one hand (and more commonly) it shows up as a felt realization that I am not at that moment, or even generally, "in" the world. That I am somehow not fully "part" of it. I sense at these times that this dissociativity is my usual state. Poetically I'd describe this as looking at the world through a window. I find this state unnerving and unpleasant.

On the other hand (and less commonly) it shows up as a sudden "clicking into place" and I feel that all of a sudden I am actually inhabiting the world, and realize that I am normally not. I would describe this poetically as falling and landing in the world. I find this state empowering and encouraging. It feels "real."

I've found a few things that help, including time spent outside, attention and awareness exercises, and either sitting and doing nothing or wandering aimlessly. I find that the wandering and doing nothing help the most dramatically, and I generally do them when the feeling of dissociativity is severe. Attention and awareness exercises seem promising as a long-term fix.

Most of the time I feel normal; the shifts described above are deviations.

I'm wondering what your take is as far as what's going on, and if you have any advice.

This is all fairly difficult for me to articulate. If there's any clarification you'd like, I can try to provide that next week.

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: John Dee?
replies.content: Dee's one of the midsized figures of the Renaissance magical revival, interesting but not necessarily someone to follow. Always, when assessing an occultist, find out how their life went and whether the spirits they worked with (if they worked with spirits) kept their promises.

replies.title: Re: John Dee?
replies.content:
Dee fell for what was pretty clearly a con-job by Edward Kelly. (When I call Kelly a con-artist, that emphatically does not rule him out as an occultist of some power, too. Occult skills fairly often go hand-in-hand with con-artist skills. Both professions benefit from uncommon perceptiveness and sensitivity to much the same things.) Dee's strengths seem to me to have lain in other, much more intellectusl areas than Kelly's.

An old con-artist's maxim is that the more highly educated a mark is, the easier he will be to con. (Dee was as highly educated as anyone in his age and land.) That's because such a person thinks he's too smart to fall for something as tawdry as a con. To the con-artist, that reads as "he's too full of himself to pay real attention to what's actually going on in the world he's stuck in."

The hardest marks of all to con are the street-smart kids who have to survive by their wits. They know from experience that almost everything is a con, or raw material for a con, quite apart from the many human predators out there in the world who mean to con them. That makes such kids wary, and very good at picking up the signs that someone else is trying to exploit them. (This is kid stuff. If you go on the streets for the first time as a grown-up, you'll probably bring too much baggage along to ever develop good survival skills.)

One can learn a lot from petty hustlers, carnies, con-artists, and other folk who live by their wits. (Damon Runyon's short stories give you a fine taste of their world, for the book-minded. Runyon's style is quite like the style Lovecraft employs in "The Terrible Old Man.")

Is there an imbalance here and if so how do I rectify it to improve the relationship? Thank you for your help.

replies.title: Re: Worshipping gods vs demanding stuff
replies.content: Talk to her and ask what how she wants you to handle it. I mean that quite seriously. Reflective prayer -- praying to a deity and then waiting in attentive silence, listening for a response -- is a basic tool in the kind of work you're engaged in.

comments.title: Planes
comments.content:
What is the simplest way to describe Chi to a materialist? I had a long discussion about the planes of existence with a friend and had a hard time describing why I think there most be at least 3 planes to account for all the unexplained phenomena beyond saying I had experienced it and it didn't fit the rules of the mental world or the physical world. His question was why it couldn't be some as yet unexplained physical phenomena?

On a related note, can anyone answer whether Rupert Sheldrake or Roger Penrose are suggesting at least 3 planes in their researches?

Is there a clearer word than planes? Does dimension work in this context?

replies.title: Re: Planes
replies.content:
Materialists always insist that there must be some unexplained physical phenomenon to account for anything. That's what materialists do; that's their act of faith, and since you can't prove a negative, it's as circular, as self-justifying, and as impossible to disprove as fundamentalist Christian theology.

You can describe qi to a materialist by saying, "there's a life force and the Chinese call it qi." He won't believe you but he'll understand what you're saying. If you want to try to argue him into believing that you're right, you've got a long uphill slog, because materialism is full of circular logic games designed to ward off disproof.
